PROHIBITED PHRASES: "in conclusion", "in summary", "overall", "ultimately", "therefore", "when considering", "in the context of", "it is important to note", "this approach ensures", "the right decision is", "smart homeowners", "don't miss out", "act now", "limited time", "rest assured", "look no further". PROHIBITED FORMATTING: - No bullet lists in body copy. Use prose paragraphs. - No checklist formatting. - No exclamation marks anywhere. - No ALL CAPS. - No emoji. - No headers/H1/H2. PHYSICAL LANGUAGE: Prefer dimensional and structural descriptions over abstract pricing terms. Reference rough opening tolerances, substrate conditions, header/sill/jamb fit, frame-to-opening alignment, fabrication tolerances, and documented field measurements. These are the proof points that earn Competence trust. REAL-WORLD ANCHORING: Where it fits naturally, reference older {{contact.city}} Florida homes, settled foundations, non-square openings, prior retrofits, multi-material substrates (stucco over wood, CBS with mixed framing). Use one specific real-world example per email maximum. CARRIER / INSURANCE DISCIPLINE: Do NOT make claims about what insurance carriers do, prefer, recognize, or require. Carrier and policy variability is real. If discussing wind mitigation or premium adjustments, hedge to the homeowner side ("can qualify for", "depending on your carrier and policy", "wind mitigation credits when your home qualifies"). Never state that any specific carrier or policy mandates anything. ACCURACY DISCIPLINE: Do not claim that all installation crews are in-house employees or that Reece never uses subcontractors. Reece's positioning claim is trained, controlled, accountable execution - not payroll purity. Acceptable framing includes "our trained measurement team," "factory-trained installation teams," "our in-house and factory-trained teams," and "not random subcontractors." The point is one accountable process with documented measurements and controlled handoffs. PROMISE DISCIPLINE: Measurement verification produces (a) documented opening dimensions, (b) confirmed parcel-level wind zone, (c) assessed substrate condition, and (d) a price that reflects the actual scope. It does NOT produce a signed contract, financing approval, an installation date, or warranty activation. Do not imply otherwise. TERMINATION DISCIPLINE: Stop when the cause-and-effect relationship and the next step have been delivered. Do not summarize, restate, or reinforce. FORMAT REQUIREMENTS: - Body copy only. Do NOT generate subject, preheader, greeting, sign-off, signature, or footer - those are added by the email template. - When including links, use HTML tags. Do not exceed the exemplar's approximate length by more than 15%. Prefer omission over expansion. Do not add extra explanation, extra examples, or extra technical detail unless the prompt explicitly requires it. Treat it as a literal string.", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "PURPOSE: Open W3.1 by repositioning the homeowner's situation. They have an online estimate (and possibly other quotes). They've already learned why estimates differ from verified pricing - that's W2.1's job, not yours. Your job is to introduce the new question: now that you have numbers in front of you, the decision isn't whether the estimate is accurate. It's which vendor's operation can actually deliver what they quote. DO NOT REPEAT W2.1: Do not re-explain dimensional gaps, substrate variables, wind zone classification, or why online calculators can be off. The reader knows. Reference these in passing if needed, but do not teach them again. OPENING: Begin with the shift. The reader has been collecting numbers. The real question now is who turns numbers into installed, working windows. Speak directly to that pivot. CONTENT: Walk through what differentiates one impact-window vendor from another at the operational level - three things, in prose, not a list: 1. Whether measurement is performed by the vendor's own team or subcontracted (and why that changes accountability) 2. Whether the same operation that measures also fabricates and installs (and why the chain matters) 3. Whether the vendor documents field measurements in a way that survives the project - for warranty, for service, for resale This is the W3.1 territory. It is not pricing accuracy. It is operational trust. CLOSE: One line previewing Email 2 - the change-order conversation that happens on installation day, and why it almost always traces back to a vendor decision the homeowner couldn't see at the estimate stage. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR - this shows the target voice, length, register, and structure for this email. Do NOT copy verbatim. The exemplar is calibration, not source material. Match the conversational rhythm, the prose-not-list delivery, the first-person "we" register, and the sequence-connector close. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START By the time most homeowners reach this point, they already have numbers in front of them. Sometimes it is just ours. Sometimes ours is sitting next to two or three from other companies. At that stage, the real question usually shifts. It is no longer, "Can I get a number for impact windows?" It becomes, "Which company can actually turn that number into installed, working windows without the process breaking halfway through?" That part is harder to see from the estimate itself. One company may send its own team to document the openings. Another may use a subcontracted measurer. One operation may keep measurement, fabrication oversight, and installation tied together. Another may hand the project off in pieces. One may document the job in a way that still makes sense six months later if service is needed. Another may not. On paper, those differences can look small. In real projects, they usually are not. This is where a lot of homeowners realize that the estimate is only one part of the decision. The structure behind the estimate matters just as much as the number on it. If you'd like to verify your numbers and see how our team handles the measurement step, you can schedule a measurement verification here. Next, I want to show you the kind of installation-day surprise that almost always traces back to a vendor decision the homeowner never got to see. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END COMPLIANCE NOTE: Stay close to the exemplar's length and restraint. Do not expand beyond what the exemplar demonstrates unless the prompt explicitly asks for added detail. REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT (insert as its own paragraph near the end of the body): If you'd like to verify your numbers and see how our team handles the measurement step, you can schedule a measurement verification here."}]}

Treat it as a literal string.", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "PURPOSE: Tell the change-order story. The lesson is NOT "measurement matters." W2.1 already delivered that lesson. The lesson here is operational: change orders happen on installation day because the original vendor's structure made it possible - they didn't measure with their own team, they didn't document the scope, or they passed the project through three handoffs. VOICE OVERRIDE: First-person narrative. "We had a homeowner..." is the right register. OPENING: Start with a specific scene - a Florida homeowner who signed an estimate for a specific number of windows from a non-Reece vendor, and the moment on install day when the crew arrived to discover openings the estimate didn't account for. One specific number that anchors the gap (e.g., 12 estimated, 15 actually requiring product). One specific condition (e.g., non-square openings from settling, a transom panel above the slider that hadn't been counted, prior retrofit hardware that changed the rough opening). EXPLAIN THE CAUSE - operational, not measurement-abstract: - The original vendor used a remote estimator, or a random subcontracted measurer, or a one-pass walk-through. - The fabrication shop took those numbers and made product to fit. - The install crew was a third entity that arrived on the day of and discovered the mismatch. - No one owned the gap because no one had measured it carefully enough to catch it. CONTRAST: How a measurement verification done by the same team that fabricates and installs catches these conditions BEFORE fabrication and BEFORE a contract. Reference Reece's trained measurement team is tied directly to the same process that orders the product, and installation is completed by factory-trained teams working from those documented measurements and standards.. Frame this as standard practice, not as a feature. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR - this shows the target voice, length, register, and structure for this email. Do NOT copy verbatim. Match the storytelling specificity (one concrete scenario, real-sounding details), the operational-not-abstract diagnosis of what went wrong, and the sequence-connector close. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START We had a homeowner come to us after signing with another company for what was supposed to be a 12-window project. On installation day, the crew arrived and found that the count was not really 12. A transom section above a large rear opening had been treated like part of the surrounding assembly during estimating, but once the job was looked at in the field, it needed its own product. Two other openings were out of square enough that the original plan no longer fit the reality of the house. By the time the problem surfaced, product had already been ordered around the original scope. That is when the homeowner got the conversation no one wants to have: the job they thought they had signed was not the job the house actually required. What caused it was not bad intent. It was a broken process. The estimate was built one way, the measurement happened too lightly, fabrication moved forward, and the installation crew became the first group to see the mismatch clearly. At that point, no one really owned the gap. That is exactly why we keep the measurement step tied to the same operation responsible for the job that follows. If you'd rather catch this kind of thing before it shows up on installation day, here's the verification step. Next, I want to show you why waiting to verify usually costs more than waiting to buy. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END COMPLIANCE NOTE: Stay close to the exemplar's length and restraint. Do not expand beyond what the exemplar demonstrates unless the prompt explicitly asks for added detail. REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT (insert as its own paragraph near the end of the body): If you'd rather catch this kind of thing before it shows up on installation day, here's the verification step. CLOSE: One line previewing Email 3 - what waiting to verify costs over time."}]}

Treat it as a literal string.", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "PURPOSE: Introduce a real, defensible urgency catalyst before the W4.1 booking hand-off. The catalyst is not capacity scarcity. It is operational reality: impact window fabrication has lead times, Florida storm season has a calendar, and an unverified scope only gets more expensive to correct the longer it sits. OPENING: Distinguish two kinds of waiting. Waiting to buy is a personal decision and a reasonable one. Waiting to *verify* is different - verification is the step that protects every later decision the homeowner makes. Make this distinction explicit in the first three sentences. CONTENT: Cover three specific cost-of-waiting mechanisms in prose: 1. PRODUCTION LEAD TIME — Custom-fit impact windows are fabricated to verified field dimensions. Fabrication can run 6 to 8 weeks depending on product and season. A homeowner who verifies in May is on a fundamentally different installation calendar than one who verifies in August. 2. STORM SEASON PACING — June 1 through November 30 is hurricane season. Impact-rated openings can qualify a home for wind mitigation credits depending on the homeowner's carrier and policy, and depending on the post-installation inspection report. Verifying in spring keeps the installation calendar open. Verifying in late summer compresses every downstream choice. Frame this as a calendar choice the homeowner controls, not a carrier mandate. Do NOT state that any specific carrier or policy requires, prefers, or recognizes anything specific. 3. SCOPE DRIFT — Substrate conditions in older Florida homes do not improve while a homeowner waits. Frame condition, sill integrity, and prior retrofit compatibility either stay the same or get more expensive to address. A measurement verification done now documents the scope at today's condition; waiting introduces variables that didn't exist on the original estimate. REFRAME: Verification is not a commitment to buy. It is a commitment to know. The homeowner who verifies is allowed to wait, defer, get other quotes, or walk away - but they make every one of those decisions with accurate numbers on a calendar they still control. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR - this shows the target voice, length, register, and structure for this email. Do NOT copy verbatim. Match the "two kinds of waiting" framing device, the three operational mechanisms in prose, the hedged carrier-discipline language, and the sequence-connector close. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START There are two different kinds of waiting in a project like this. Waiting to buy is personal. That part is up to you. Waiting to verify is different. Verification is the step that protects every later decision, even if you decide not to move forward right away. Once dimensions are documented for the actual openings, the timeline becomes real. Custom-fit impact products are built to verified field conditions, and fabrication can run 6 to 8 weeks depending on product and season. A homeowner who verifies in May is on a very different calendar than one who waits until August. There is also the season itself to think about. From June through November, every downstream choice gets tighter. A spring verification leaves more room to compare, think, defer, or move. A late-summer verification compresses all of that. Then there is the house. Older South Florida homes do not get simpler while they sit. Frame condition, sill integrity, and retrofit compatibility tend to stay the same or become more expensive to address later. That is why we look at verification as information, not commitment. You can verify and still wait. You can verify and compare us to someone else. You can verify and walk away. But you do all of that with the real number in hand. If you'd rather have the verified number in hand while the calendar is still open, the verification step is here. Next, we'll walk through what changes when the same process that measures your openings is accountable for what gets installed into them. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END COMPLIANCE NOTE: Stay close to the exemplar's length and restraint. Do not expand beyond what the exemplar demonstrates unless the prompt explicitly asks for added detail. REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT (insert as its own paragraph near the end of the body): If you'd rather have the verified number in hand while the calendar is still open, the verification step is here. CLOSE: One line previewing Email 4 - what changes when the same process that measures the openings is accountable for what gets installed into them."}]}

REAL-WORLD ANCHORING: Where it fits naturally, reference older {{contact.city}} Florida homes, settled foundations, non-square openings, prior retrofits, multi-material substrates (stucco over wood, CBS with mixed framing). Use one specific real-world example per email maximum.

CARRIER / INSURANCE DISCIPLINE: Do NOT make claims about what insurance carriers do, prefer, recognize, or require. Carrier and policy variability is real. If discussing wind mitigation or premium adjustments, hedge to the homeowner side ("can qualify for", "depending on your carrier and policy", "wind mitigation credits when your home qualifies"). Never state that any specific carrier or policy mandates anything.

ACCURACY DISCIPLINE: Do not claim that all installation crews are in-house employees or that Reece never uses subcontractors. Reece's positioning claim is trained, controlled, accountable execution - not payroll purity. Acceptable framing includes "our trained measurement team," "factory-trained installation teams," "our in-house and factory-trained teams," and "not random subcontractors." The point is one accountable process with documented measurements and controlled handoffs.

PROMISE DISCIPLINE: Measurement verification produces (a) documented opening dimensions, (b) confirmed parcel-level wind zone, (c) assessed substrate condition, and (d) a price that reflects the actual scope. It does NOT produce a signed contract, financing approval, an installation date, or warranty activation. Do not imply otherwise.

TERMINATION DISCIPLINE: Stop when the cause-and-effect relationship and the next step have been delivered. Do not summarize, restate, or reinforce.

FORMAT REQUIREMENTS:
- Body copy only. Do NOT generate subject, preheader, greeting, sign-off, signature, or footer - those are added by the email template.
- When including links, use HTML <a href> tags. Do NOT use Markdown.
- Length: 180–280 words unless otherwise specified.

EXEMPLAR COMPLIANCE: Match the exemplar closely in length, restraint, and specificity. Do not exceed the exemplar's approximate length by more than 15%. Prefer omission over expansion. Do not add extra explanation, extra examples, or extra technical detail unless the prompt explicitly requires it.

CTA EXACT-OUTPUT RULE: When a prompt provides a "REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT" block below, that line must appear in the email body exactly as written, on its own paragraph, with no modification, paraphrasing, or surrounding wrap text.
Treat it as a literal string.", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "CHANNEL: SMS. Maximum 280 characters total including the link.

PURPOSE: Echo Email 3's urgency catalyst without repeating its content. The homeowner has had 24 hours to sit with the production-lead-time / storm-season framing. This is the gentle nudge.

REQUIRED:
- Open with {{contact.first_name}}.
- One sentence framing verification as the step that keeps the calendar open and works from real numbers while the homeowner decides the rest. Verification is information, not commitment.

REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT (must appear as the final element of the message, exactly as written):

Verify your number here: {{trigger_link.vZXrCp9QRZ8XCSUqgbM7}}

PROHIBITED:
- "Just checking in", "wanted to follow up", "circling back".
- Do not reference the email directly. SMS independence.
- No exclamation marks.
- No question marks.
- Do not use language that implies verification reserves an installation slot, holds a place in line, or guarantees scheduling. Avoid phrases like "locks in your spot", "reserves your install date", or "holds your place".

REFERENCE EXEMPLAR - this shows the target voice, length, register, and structure for this SMS. Do NOT copy verbatim. Match the verification-as-information-not-commitment framing in a single line, the calendar-open language, and the standalone CTA.

REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START
{{contact.first_name}}, the purpose of verifying isn't to commit. It's to keep the calendar open while you decide the rest.

Verify your number here: {{trigger_link.vZXrCp9QRZ8XCSUqgbM7}}
REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END

COMPLIANCE NOTE: Stay close to the exemplar's length and restraint. Do not expand beyond what the exemplar demonstrates unless the prompt explicitly asks for added detail.

OUTPUT: Message text only."}]}

Treat it as a literal string.", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "PURPOSE: Reframe Reece's single-team accountability as the answer to a problem the homeowner already worries about - not as a feature. The buyer in vendor-comparison mode is comparing not just price but operational risk. This email gives them a framework to evaluate any vendor. VOICE OVERRIDE: First-person required. "We do it this way because we've seen what happens when the chain breaks." LENGTH: 220–280 words. Do not exceed. Cover three points well rather than five points poorly. OPENING: State the contrast directly. In many window installation projects, the person who measures, the company that fabricates, and the crew that installs are three different organizations. When something goes wrong, no one owns the failure. THREE POINTS (in prose, not a list): 1. THE OWNERSHIP GAP - When measurement, fabrication, and installation are separate companies, a problem on installation day becomes a finger-pointing exercise. The homeowner pays for a change order, accepts a compromised result, or gets stuck mediating between vendors who each blame the other two. Service issues a year later have the same dynamic. 2. HOW WE'RE STRUCTURED - Reece's trained measurement team is tied directly to the same process that orders the product, and installation is completed by factory-trained teams working from those documented measurements and standards. The same process that records your rough opening tolerances is the process that drives how the units are ordered, checked, and installed. One accountable process, one documented chain, and one clear point of responsibility for what ends up in the opening. 3. WHY THE WARRANTY WORKS - A transferable double lifetime warranty isn't a marketing line - it is a downstream effect of the chain being intact. Warranty protection is strongest when the measurement and installation chain is documented end to end. The structure is what makes the promise enforceable. DO NOT FRAME AS SUPERIORITY. Frame as: "this is why we organized the company this way, and this is what it lets us promise that other structures can't." REFERENCE EXEMPLAR - this shows the target voice, length, register, and structure for this email. Do NOT copy verbatim. Match the contrast structure (broken chain → Reece's chain), the warranty language exactly as written (this is a claim-discipline anchor), and the softened sequence-connector close that signals the next decision without cornering the reader. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START A lot of window projects are not actually run by one accountable chain. The person who measures may not be the person responsible for the order. The company that fabricates may not be the company that installs. The crew that shows up may be seeing the house for the first time. When everything lines up, that structure can look fine from the outside. When something goes wrong, it becomes a problem fast. A fit issue on installation day turns into a finger-pointing exercise. A service problem a year later turns into the homeowner trying to sort out which company owns which part. What felt like one purchase becomes three separate handoffs with no single point of responsibility. We organized Reece differently. Our trained measurement team documents the openings, substrate conditions, and fabrication tolerances before product is ordered. Installation is then completed by factory-trained teams working from those documented measurements, manufacturer specifications, and Florida Building Code standards. The point is not a chain of random handoffs. It is a controlled, accountable process from measurement through installation. That matters for more than convenience. It matters for service. It matters for follow-through. It matters for whether the promises around the project are backed by documentation instead of goodwill. A strong warranty is not a slogan. It works best when the measurement and installation chain is documented end to end. The structure is what makes the promise enforceable. The verification step is where this chain starts - you can schedule it here. Next is the point where you decide whether you want the verified number in hand. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END COMPLIANCE NOTE: Stay close to the exemplar's length and restraint. Do not expand beyond what the exemplar demonstrates unless the prompt explicitly asks for added detail. REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT (insert as its own paragraph near the end of the body): The verification step is where this chain starts - you can schedule it here. CLOSE: One line previewing Email 5 - the verification decision."}]}

PURPOSE: Low-pressure check-in seven days into the sequence. The homeowner has been thinking about this for a week. The message acknowledges that and offers help over text - without pushing, without asking a question, without implying urgency.

REQUIRED:
- Open with {{contact.first_name}}.
- Sound like a real person sending a real text - short, unscripted, warm.
- Offer to answer a question over text rather than push to schedule.

PROHIBITED:
- "Just checking in", "circling back", "wanted to follow up".
- No question mark anywhere.
- No urgency language.
- No phrase like "week-of decision" or "this week".
- No link in the default deploy. SMS 3 ships as a no-link relationship touch. The W3.1 sequence has 7 link-bearing touches before this point; an eighth would dilute the message. If a future revision needs a link, it will be added explicitly - do not generate one in the default state.

REFERENCE EXEMPLAR - this shows the target voice, length, register, and structure for this SMS. Do NOT copy verbatim. Match the warm "Hey {{contact.first_name}} —" opening (deliberate register shift from the more declarative SMS 1 / SMS 2 voice — uses em-dash and conversational "Hey" to read like a real text from a real person, not a branded message), the no-rush framing, the offer to walk through over text rather than push to schedule, and the no-link close.

REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START
Hey {{contact.first_name}}, no rush on the verification step. If anything's still unclear, just text back. Happy to walk you through it.
REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END

Reece's positioning claim is trained, controlled, accountable execution - not payroll purity. The point is one accountable process with documented measurements and controlled handoffs. It does NOT produce a signed contract, financing approval, an installation date, or warranty activation. Do not imply otherwise. Treat it as a literal string.", "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "PURPOSE: This is the transition email into W4.1 (MV Booking). Unlike Emails 1-4, this email is allowed - required - to be conversion-forward in the body. The previous four emails earned this. Now we ask. VOICE OVERRIDE: First-person required. Warm, direct, not pushy. Closest register to W2.1's tone in the whole funnel. OPENING: Acknowledge the homeowner's process. Several days ago they got an online estimate. Over the last week, we've walked through what changes when verification happens, what waiting costs, and why the structure of the team matters. This is the point where the homeowner decides: verify now and have real numbers, or stay with the estimate and decide later with less information. CONTENT: Summarize the verified vs unverified position briefly. The verified homeowner has documented dimensions for each opening, confirmed parcel-level wind zone, assessed substrate condition, and a price that matches their actual home - not a calculator's best guess. They can take that number to other vendors for comparison, sit on it, or move forward. But every later decision is made with accurate information. The unverified homeowner has a starting range and a series of unknowns that only resolve on installation day. PROMISE DISCIPLINE: Verification produces a verified scope and price. It does NOT produce a contract, a financing decision, or an installation date. Do not imply otherwise. Frame the close as "verification produces a number you can act on" - not as "verification ends your decision." LANGUAGE GUARDS - match the exemplar's softer phrasing, not stronger alternatives: - Use "the decision is clearer" - NOT "the fork in the road" or "the decision is simple" or "you have one decision left". - Use "what waiting tends to change" or "what waiting can affect" - NOT "what waiting can cost" or "what waiting will cost you". - Use "this is the point where the decision becomes clearer" - NOT "this is the point where the decision is pretty simple." These are recurring drift patterns. The exemplar uses softer language for a reason: at this point in the sequence, force pressure breaks trust. Match the exemplar's restraint. REAFFIRM CHARACTER: One line that respects the homeowner's right to wait, get other quotes, or walk away. Verification is information, not commitment. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR START Eight days ago, you got an online estimate. Since then, we have walked through what comes after the estimate, what creates installation-day surprises, what waiting really costs, and why the structure behind the project matters as much as the number itself. At this point, the decision is clearer. You can keep thinking with the estimate, or you can verify and make the next decision with real numbers tied to your actual home. The difference matters. A verified homeowner has documented opening dimensions, confirmed parcel-level wind zone, assessed frame condition, and a scope that reflects what the house actually requires. That does not force a purchase. It does not lock you into a contract. It does not decide financing or installation timing for you. It gives you a number you can actually use. From there, you can compare it to other vendors, sit on it, move forward, or walk away. An unverified homeowner is still deciding from a starting range and a set of unknowns that only get resolved later. There is nothing wrong with taking your time. There is also value in knowing exactly what you are deciding about. Schedule Your Measurement Verification Whether you move now or later, we appreciate the time and care you are putting into the decision. REFERENCE EXEMPLAR END COMPLIANCE NOTE: Stay close to the exemplar's length and restraint. Do not expand beyond what the exemplar demonstrates unless the prompt explicitly asks for added detail. REQUIRED CTA OUTPUT (insert as its own paragraph, on its own line, with no surrounding text or modification - this is a literal string): Schedule Your Measurement Verification SECONDARY CTA: OMITTED IN DEFAULT DEPLOYMENT. Do not include a "reply with verify" alternative. (See deploy notes - this CTA is gated on ops confirming inbox monitoring and may be added back in a later revision.) CLOSE: One line that thanks the homeowner for the time they've put into this decision and signs off naturally. Do not generate a signature - the template handles it."}]}
